dialogue.utterance: Unit 2, dispatch for unconscious female, possible seizure activity at 824 Adams Street.
Hi, I'm John, an EMT with the rescue squad. Can you tell me what made you call 911?
She was found unconscious in a closet. When she woke up, she was agitated and said she had chest pain.
She was unresponsive at first but responded to touch. No full assessment has been completed yet.
She opened her eyes when I touched her shoulder, but didn't respond with words.
She began flailing her arms when I tried to check her pulse.
Airway appears patent; she's self-maintaining at this time.
Initial breathing rate is 12, no signs of distress.
Respiratory rate remains at 12.
Blood pressure is 137 over 85.
Pulse is 115, well palpable though fast.
Lung sounds are clear bilaterally; no signs of bilateral obstruction.
Skin is warm, dry, and intact at this time; will reassess after Narcan.
Oxygen saturation is at 99% with ambient air.
She complained of chest pain when she woke up but was not short of breath and denies dizziness.
She recently left jail and has a history of opioid, cocaine, and sedative, including hypnotic and anxiolytic abuse.
She's allergic to penicillin.
I don't have any information on her medications, though she uses drugs regularly.
She hasn't responded verbally yet but did open her eyes and move upon touch.
Pupils are 1 mm, and sluggish to respond upon initial assessment.
Chest is symmetrical, breath sounds clear, and heart without murmurs or rubs.
Blood sugar is zero via fingerstick.
Based on signs and medical history, we'll follow protocol 7-1 for opioid overdose and poisoning.
We'll establish an IV for medication administration.
IV started in her right hand after one attempt, secured properly.
Administering 200 ml of normal saline. Patient is ready for Narcan.
Administering 0.5 mg of Narcan intravenously to reverse opioid effects.
Respirations have increased to 24.
Blood pressure is now 128 over 67.
Oxygen saturation is at 91% on ambient air.
I'm initiating 15 liters per minute via non-rebreather mask to optimize oxygenation.
Her pulse is stable at 116.
Blood sugar is now 109.
I'll switch her to nasal cannula at 4 LPM for more tolerable O2 delivery.
Pulse is now 131-elevating but stable.
Respiratory rate has slowed to 14-a good trend.
Blood pressure is now 142 over 69.
Her oxygen saturation is back to 99%.
Blood sugar remains at 0-will consider repeating it if it continues.
Placing patient on 4-lead ECG monitor now.
12-lead ECG completed. Findings show sinus tachycardia and a short PR interval.
VCU received the report of tachycardia and overdose signs. They're expecting her.
Patient is now on the Reeves stretcher and secured in semi-Fowler's position.
She's transported via stretcher with five seat belts. No movement during transport.
Delay expected due to scene extrication and decontamination at the hospital.
This is a 29-year-old female with probable opioid poisoning, sinus tachycardia, and seizure-like activity initially.
Blood pressure increased to 142/69 after Narcan. She's on nasal cannula at 4 LPM and 200 ml normal saline given.
Her reported past medical history includes opioid, cocaine, and sedative, including hypnotic and anxiolytic abuse. Allergies listed: penicillin.
Skin became diaphoretic after Narcan. She has a minor laceration on the right plantar foot. No major injuries. VCU is aware and ready.
Handoff is with Ellen Midyette, RN. No other complications during transport or scene.
Glasgow Coma Scale increased to 8 prior to intervention, improved but still somnolent after Narcan.
No jugular venous distention noted; trachea is midline.
Abdomen is soft, non-tender, non-distended.
Chest wall intact, bilateral breath sounds clear.
No significant trauma. One small laceration noted on right plantar foot.
No rectal bleeding noted. No urination reported during assessment.
She was moved from the residence to the stretcher with mechanical lift assistance due to small space for extrication.
Rechecked IV access and medication administration; both remain patent and effective.
Blood sugar still 0-patient not eating recently, likely due to substance use.
Skin now diaphoretic but intact; no signs of jaundice, purpura, or rashes.
She stumbled into the house, then clenched up and experienced jerking and shaking while in a chair. Fell off the chair and was found unconscious.
No chest deformity or asymmetry in expansion.
EMS line is thin, she is ready for transport to VCU in semi-Fowler's positioning.
Five-point restraints confirmed on hospital stretcher. Draw sheet method used for transfer in ER.
EKG findings reflect sustained sinus tachycardia, PR interval short. Noted in ECG strip.
Police and fire involved in extrication at scene-removed with mechanical assist due to narrow closet.
Final transfer was smooth with draw sheet without visible discomfort.
Inhalation status: No aspiration risk observed; no GI motor function compromise.
Pain rating initially 4, no improvement after Narcan but consciousness improved, according to patient.
History includes multiple substance abuse: opioids, cocaine, sedatives, including hypnotics and anxiolytics.
Vitals stabilized on nasal cannula and IV fluids. EKG complete and on file for hospital RN pickup.
Patient reports chest pressure; no new symptoms during last 30 minutes.
No prior documented intake of food or fluids. Bystander estimated recent episode occurs 10-15 minutes pre-discovery.
No further complications occurred en route. ER staff arrived and took full patient report from us.
Final assessment of the GCS was 8 before Narcan, now awake and verbalizing, albeit confused.
Continuous ECG readout used for all transport vitals ensuring consistent monitoring.
She likely experienced a seizure episode while seated, with possible drug overdose. No confirmed spinal involvement.
Hospital bed awaiting patient before ED arrival. No neuro signs to report-except transient confusion.
Final stabilization achieved: patient now awake, breathing stable, and breathing oxygen by nasal cannula.
No change in neuro status reported; patient began moving extremities spontaneously before Narcan administration.
All documentation complete per VCU protocol. Vitals per time-stamped log transmitted now.
No sharp objects or weapons reported on scene.
Police secured the scene prior to our departure.
No signs of trauma that may require orthopedic evaluation at this time.
Noted on PCR and discussed with hospital for possible lab testing on arrival.
No risk of scene or patient violence reported by bystander or upon arrival.
VCU is aware of the likely cause-opioid overdose with secondary seizure, abusing multiple classes of drugs.
Transportation delayed about 20 minutes due to space constraints, but uncomplicated transfer now.
All protocols followed including Narcan administration and IV cold fluids for initial stabilization.
No IV attempt complication reported. Fluid administration completed at 200 ml.
No signs of IV infiltration or patient movement complications during transport.
Decontamination process at VCU complete, patient now stabilized.
Take care of her, thanks.
Referred to primary care in ER for further evaluation given the medical history and current status.
nice case, handled efficiently given the scene size and multifactorial situation.
Thanks for the backup call.
All good from here. Good luck.
